fact: March 11 sits exactly 10 days before the spring equinox, making it the perfect 'almost spring' day when winter starts losing its grip in the Northern Hemisphere!
In the Northern Hemisphere, daylight stretches about 2 minutes longer each day around this time - that's like gaining a whole extra hour of sunlight every month!
Skunks typically wake up from their winter sleepiness right around now in northern climates and start wandering around looking for mates - which means way more stinky encounters!
Tree sap runs fastest during these mid-March days in maple-growing regions because the nights are still freezing but the days warm up - perfect for making maple syrup!
Early tree pollen often starts floating through the air around March 11 in temperate regions, even before you can see any green buds - which explains why some people feel sneezy!
March 11 usually falls smack in the middle of 'mud season' in cold places - when all that melting snow turns the ground into a giant, squishy mess!
Earthworms typically start wiggling up to the surface around this date in northern climates because the soil finally thaws enough for them to move around and breathe!
The sun rises almost exactly due east and sets due west around March 11, making it super easy to figure out directions without a compass!
March 11 marks day 70 of the year - which means you've already lived through more than 19% of the entire year!
In the Southern Hemisphere, March 11 often signals the start of serious autumn weather, with leaves beginning their colorful transformation!
Hibernating animals like bears and groundhogs are typically stirring awake around now in northern regions because their internal clocks sense the changing daylight hours!
March 11 sits in the zodiac sign Pisces, represented by two fish swimming in opposite directions - pretty fitting for a day caught between winter and spring!
